focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Agnes Chen's roommate breathed in nine-second cycles. There was a wet click on the inhale, then a pause, then the drain on the exhale. The interval varied from seven to eleven seconds. Agnes had logged the numbers in her head for two months before accepting that the variation was random. It was not pathological. It was a pharmacist's reflex: measure, correlate, intervene. There was nothing to intervene on. Her roommate, Doris Okonkwo, eighty-three, was five years into Alzheimer's and had not produced a recognizable word since Christmas two years ago. The breathing meant only that Doris's body was still running its autonomic functions while the person who had lived inside it was gone.

It was Sunday. Helen was coming.

Agnes lay facing the wall with her eyes open. She had been awake since five-fifteen. She was always awake by five-fifteen. Her body kept pharmacist's hours; she had opened the Walgreens in Edison, New Jersey, at six AM five days a week for twenty-seven of her forty-two years behind the counter, and the internal clock had not adjusted for retirement, stroke, or institutionalization. The suprachiasmatic nucleus, she knew, was largely immune to changes in circumstance. You could strip away everything else and the body would still wake at five-fifteen and expect you to count pills.

The Sunday morning aide was Shanice, early twenties, efficient, talked while she worked. She came in at seven-forty and changed Doris's incontinence brief with competent hands, narrating as she moved: how's your morning, Mrs. O, let's get you cleaned up, there we go. Agnes gave Shanice credit for this. Most aides talked to the wall or to their phones. Shanice talked to Doris. It was a small kindness. It cost nothing. It was still rare.

To Agnes, without slowing down:...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...if he was eating, saving him half a dinner roll in a napkin for Thursday. He pressed his thumb into the phone hinge and said nothing.

"You eat dinner?" Lola asked.

"Yeah. I had — yeah, I ate."

"Cereal isn't dinner, Marcus."

He laughed, a small, involuntary sound that surprised him. "How'd you know?"

"Because I know you. Go make something real. There's rice, you can make rice. Everybody can make rice."

"I'll make rice."

"With something. Rice with something."

"Rice with something. I promise."

He could hear her smile through the phone. "Okay. Goodnight, mijo. Be safe."

"Goodnight, Lola."

He sat on the couch after she hung up. Then he got up anyway and went to the kitchenette because standing up felt close enough to obedience to count for something. The cabinet over the sink held a ten-pound bag of rice, one can of black beans, garlic powder, a packet of sazon Lola had bullied him into buying because plain rice, she'd said, was hospital food. He touched the bag and left it there. The sink held one bowl, one fork, one pan with dried egg stuck to the bottom. The apartment was quiet except for the boiler in the utility closet on the other side of the wall, which ticked and rumbled at irregular intervals like a mechanical heartbeat with an arrhythmia. Upstairs, his landlord's television murmured. The ceiling was low enough that he could hear the floorboards creak when the landlord's wife walked to the kitchen.

He did not make rice. He went back to the couch with the prepaid phone in his hand and the CNA certificate on the wall staring back at him. Upstairs, his landlord's wife crossed the kitchen again. Marcus bent forward until his elbows hit his knees and stayed there under the low ceiling while the boiler ticked and Lola's voice kept saying *Rice with something.*
